heading: Rows Come From Cases
body: A **truth table** lists the possible truth-value assignments for the variables in a formula. Each row represents one possible assignment. [page:1]

examples.text: If a formula uses only $P$, the table must cover the cases where $P$ is true and where $P$ is false. [page:1]
If a formula uses $P$ and $Q$, the table must cover all combinations of truth values for $P$ and $Q$. [page:1]

heading: Use $2^n$
body: The **row formula** is $2^n$, where $n$ is the number of unique propositional variables. This works because each variable has two possible truth values. [page:1]

heading: Count Unique Letters
body: **Unique variables** are distinct statement letters, counted once each. Repeated letters do not increase the number of rows.

examples.text: In $(P \vee Q) \rightarrow R$, the unique variables are $P$, $Q$, and $R$.
In $(P \wedge P) \rightarrow Q$, the unique variables are $P$ and $Q$, not three variables.
$\sim P$ has 1 unique variable, so it needs 2 rows. [page:1]
$(P \wedge Q)$ has 2 unique variables, so it needs 4 rows. [page:1]
$((P \vee Q) \rightarrow R)$ has 3 unique variables, so it needs 8 rows. [page:1]

heading: Do Not Overcount
body: A common mistake is counting operators or repeated letters as new variables. Only distinct propositional letters determine $n$.

examples.text: $(P \wedge Q) \vee P$ has 3 variables because it has three letters written.
$(P \wedge Q) \vee P$ has 2 unique variables, so it needs 4 rows. [page:1]

heading: Core Method
body: Find the number of unique propositional variables. Then compute $2^n$ to get the required number of truth-table rows. [page:1]
